Title: Wide Character Identifiers
Description: Virtuoso traditionally stores all schema identifiers using 8-bit character fields for compatibility and performance.
Two mechanisms exist to support non-ASCII identifier names:
1) Maintain 8-bit identifier storage:
- Identifiers are stored and retrieved using the database character set.
- Supports languages with single-byte encodings (Western European, Cyrillic).
- Does not support multi-byte character sets (e.g., East Asian languages).
- Does not allow identifiers containing characters from multiple encodings.
2) Store all identifiers as UTF-8 encoded Unicode:
- Allows any Unicode character to be used in identifiers.
- UTF-8 uses variable-length encoding, which affects LIKE comparisons.
UTF-8 behavior is controlled using the INI configuration flag SQL_UTF8_EXECS = 1/0 in the [Client] section.
- SQL_UTF8_EXECS = 1 → UTF-8 identifier storage enabled.
- SQL_UTF8_EXECS = 0 → Disabled (default).
NOTE: Changing SQL_UTF8_EXECS after storing identifiers may make previously stored identifiers unreadable by normal means.
SQL parsing behavior:
- Incoming SQL statements are expanded to Unicode before parsing.
- SQL literals are interpreted correctly under UTF-8 handling.
- Identifiers are stored and compared in UTF-8 inside system tables.
Identifier retrieval:
- Returned identifiers are translated from UTF-8 to Unicode for the client.
- Clients normally do not receive raw UTF-8 data.
UTF-8 does not affect SQL syntax rule parsing, as SQL special characters remain ASCII.
upper() and lower() SHOULD NOT be applied directly to UTF-8 identifiers:
- UTF-8 encoded identifiers must first be converted to wide strings via charset_recode,
- then converted to upper/lower case,
- then encoded back to UTF-8 using charset_recode.
LIKE pattern matching:
- Single-character LIKE patterns do not work reliably for UTF-8 identifiers because 1 character can require up to 6 bytes.
- ASCII characters are the only safe exception.
